title: A Resource-Driven Framework for Configurable Entanglement in Quantum Networks
authors: Francesco Mazza, Claudio Pellitteri, Angela Sara Cacciapuoti, Marcello Caleffi
abstract: Shared multipartite entanglement defines a ``whatever channel'', i.e., a latent communication substrate that does not determine a priori which end-to-end entangled links are activated, but can be configured to support different entanglement-connectivity graphs through Local Operations and Classical Communication (LOCC). Building on this, we propose a resource-driven framework in which multipartite entanglement is treated as a programmable resource that induces a space of admissible entanglement-graph configurations. Within this framework, connectivity provisioning emerges as a particular instance of a more general resource reconfiguration process. To support this paradigm, we introduce a set of structural design parameters that characterize the operational degrees of freedom of the resource and define the admissible transformations independently of the specific mechanism used to realize them. We then formalize Entanglement Rolling as a measurement-based protocol that operates over the induced configuration space, enabling the systematic reconfiguration of the shared resource across a family of multipartite states. Finally, we analyze the proposed framework under realistic noise conditions. Leveraging the Noisy Stabilizer Formalism (NSF), we derive closed-form noise maps that characterize the effect of noise on the resource transformations and show that the proposed approach maintains reliable performance under relevant noise processes.
summary: The paper proposes a new way to use multipartite entanglement in quantum networks by treating it as a programmable substrate that can be reconfigured into different network topologies. By using a protocol called 'Entanglement Rolling,' users can dynamically create Bell pairs or GHZ states from a shared resource. The study demonstrates that this method is efficient and robust against common types of quantum noise.
main_problem: Developing a resource-driven framework to treat multipartite entanglement as a programmable 'whatever channel' that can be reconfigured into various entanglement-connectivity graphs via LOCC.
main_result: The introduction of the 'Entanglement Rolling' protocol, which maximizes the number of concurrent Bell pairs extracted from a GTL resource state, and a proof that this approach remains resilient under realistic depolarizing and dephasing noise.
method: A mathematical framework using graph states and the Noisy Stabilizer Formalism (NSF) to derive closed-form noise maps and analyze the reconfiguration of resource states through Pauli measurements.
model_or_system: A hierarchical quantum network model consisting of Tier-2 orchestration nodes and Tier-1 peer nodes, utilizing Generalized Tree-like (GTL) graph states as the shared entanglement resource.
key_observables: Entanglement fidelity (F), number of extractable Bell pairs/GHZ states, and peer proximity.
important_parameters: Bridge rank (r), bridge degree (kappa_b), depolarizing noise parameter (p), and dephasing time (T).
main_assumptions: The resource states are restricted to two-colorable GTL graph states, and noise is modeled as independent single-qubit Pauli noise.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the three-tier reconfiguration process; Figure 2 depicts the hierarchical network model; Figure 3 illustrates the Entanglement Rolling effect; Figure 4 shows the two-stage extraction process; Figure 5 visualizes noise impact on rolling; Figure 6 presents heatmaps and curves of fidelity thresholds.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the resource-driven framework and the 'whatever channel' concept, introduces the GTL graph state model and structural parameters, formalizes the Entanglement Rolling protocol and its topological effects, provides a noise analysis using the Noisy Stabilizer Formalism, and concludes with numerical validation of fidelity under realistic noise.
why_interesting: It provides a systematic way to manage multipartite entanglement as a programmable resource, which is highly relevant for the development of scalable quantum internet architectures and the control of distributed quantum information.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: High level representation of our research problem. The generation and distribution of a multipartite resource state (1) establishes a whatever channel between a subset of network nodes (2). By applying LOCC operations on the shared resource state, entanglement can be reconfigured and shared between a subset of network nodes (3), for instance, parallel communication resources can be extracted through the resolution of the whatever channel.

Fig. 2: Pictorial representation of the system model and node hierarchy. Tier2 node(s) retains no orchestration qubits Vo of a shared two-colorable graph state, while the κ peer qubits Vc are distributed to Tier1 nodes.
Fig. 3: Illustration of the Entanglement Rolling effect induced by a Pauli X measurement on orchestration qubit oi of a GTL resource state (G, ˆκb = 2) with arbitrary length.

Fig. 4: Example resource state extraction using the Entangle- ment Rolling procedure on a GTL resource state (G, ˆκb = 3): a configurable sequence of Pauli X measurements at Tier2, followed by local Pauli Z measurements at the Tier1 end- node level, extracts two independent entangled resources (LU equivalent to 3-qubit GHZ states).

Fig. 5: Example of (a) Entanglement Rolling with noisy GTL graph state (G, ˆκb = 2). Node labels denote Tier1 nodes; repeated labels indicate that the same Tier1 node holds multiple peer qubits, as the general case discussed in Sec. III. Pauli X measurements at Tier2, followed by local Pauli-Z measurements at Tier1, isolate independent Bell pairs under depolarizing noise (b) Fidelity of the extracted Bell pairs vs. the depolarizing parameter p (one curve per extracted pair).
